text: In hot climates, keep fresh food at 4°C, use insulated containers, and consume within 24 hours to prevent rapid bacterial growth.
For humid conditions, store vegetables in breathable bags in the refrigerator. Wait to wash them until directly before use to reduce mold risks.
Store dried Ayurvedic herbs in airtight glass containers in a cool, dark place away from direct sunlight. Moisture and heat degrade active compounds.
Biological samples should be stored immediately at -20°C. Ensure rapid transport using dry ice and document every temperature excursion.
Unpasteurized milk must be kept between 1°C and 4°C and boiled immediately upon reaching the destination.
During transport, medicinal herbs should be sealed in multi-layer moisture barrier bags. Avoid refrigeration changes greater than 5°C to prevent condensation accumulation inside the packaging.
Cold chain products (dairy, meat, produce) must maintain continuous refrigeration below 5°C. Use data loggers to record temperature during the entire transport journey.
Laboratory bio-samples suspected of contamination should be double-bagged in biohazard-safe containers and transported on ice at 4°C to a certified lab immediately.
Grains and dry food products should be stored in sealed silos or containers with moisture below 13%. Use desiccants and monitor humidity sensors regularly.
Expired or contaminated batches must be quarantined and destroyed as per local bio-safety regulations. Document disposal with photographic evidence and regulatory clearance numbers.
Essential oil extracts from herbs should be stored in dark amber glass bottles at 10–15°C, away from UV light. Oxidation significantly degrades potency within weeks of exposure to air.
